kicking that document given the query x and then this p-theta x-z and all the previous tokens is

So you can see that this is basically linearly interpolating between the multiple documents.
And if we look this can be considered the retriever and the generator or the retriever and the reader.
One really important thing here that enables end-to-end training is they have this probability of
the retriever be based on embeddings. And so here we have the document embedded and the query
embedded and the probability is proportional to the inner product of these x-benedgated. So you're
basically taking a softmax over the inner product between the two. And this adjusts the retriever
to give higher similarities to helpful documents. And so because the probability of the retriever
model here is included in the end-to-end probability you don't actually need any annotations about
which documents are useful. You can just train all of this end-to-end and let back-prop do its thing
to update the retriever as well. One important issue when training models like this is that
the search index will be fun to steal. So what do I mean by this? If we go back to our previous
thing about dense models, creating this blue search index on the right side of the figure here
is very costly. So like let's say you want to embed a million documents or a billion documents
if you're a big search engine company. So doing this is very slow. And in contrast doing
lookup with these approximate nearest neighbor searches is sub linear time or even log time. So you
can do it relatively quickly. So it's fine to do lookup over this big index. But if you start
updating this document embedding you need to recreate the entire index and that would be
very computationally costly. So the solution to this proposed in this rare paper by Lewis
et al. is we only train the query embeddings and we keep the document embedding specs.

and in that method they basically had an asynchronous process that was going through and using
the most recent document embedder to re update the search index during training. But that is

So there's a bunch of different methods. The rare paper that I mentioned before
did this only once right at the beginning of generation. It grabbed a single document and then
generated the entire output. This is the default method used by most systems. However there's
other options as well. You can retrieve several times during generation is necessary.

And the way this was trained is training was done in an iterative manner where it basically generated
examples of tools being useful. And when the tools improve the probability of the following
output, then that would be treated as a positive example and used to further train the model.
